Momentum in the S&P 500

A Quintile Analysis, 1993–2025

Author

Jack Simmons

Published

March 14, 2026

Introduction

Price momentum — the tendency for assets that have performed well recently to continue outperforming — is one of the most replicated findings in empirical finance. Jegadeesh and Titman (1993) documented it in U.S. equities; it has since been found across asset classes and geographies.

This analysis examines momentum at the index level using the S&P 500 (proxied by SPY from its 1993 inception). Rather than asking whether a momentum strategy beats buy-and-hold — an unfair contest during a 30-year bull market — we ask a more precise question:

Within the distribution of monthly momentum signals observed since 1993, do months with high signals predict meaningfully higher forward returns than months with low signals?

The framework is quintile analysis: for each metric, we bin all monthly observations into five equal groups and measure mean forward returns per group. The Q5–Q1 spread is our primary measure of signal efficacy.

Data

SPY adjusted-close daily prices from January 1993 to present, sourced via yfinance. Adjusted close accounts for dividends and splits, approximating a total return series.

Show code 
import sys
import os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath("momentum_analysis.qmd")))

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use("Agg")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from IPython.display import Image, display

from fetch_data import fetch
from momentum import compute_all
from analytics import (
    align_monthly, forward_returns,
    spread_stat, information_coefficient,
    multi_signal_tranche,
)
from backtest import (
    spread_table, quintile_analysis,
    plot_quintile_bars, plot_spread_heatmap,
    plot_tranche_heatmap, plot_rolling_spread,
)

prices_df = fetch()
prices = prices_df["price"].squeeze()
metrics = compute_all(prices)
fwd = forward_returns(prices)

print(f"Daily observations : {len(prices):,}")
print(f"Date range         : {prices.index[0].date()} to {prices.index[-1].date()}")
print(f"Monthly obs (fwd)  : {len(fwd):,}")
Downloading SPY from 1993-01-01...
Saved 8337 rows to data\spy_daily.csv
Daily observations : 8,337
Date range         : 1993-01-29 to 2026-03-13
Monthly obs (fwd)  : 387

Momentum Metrics

Eight metrics are computed from the daily price series. They fall into three families:

Metric Formula Intuition
mom_12_1 Return(t–12m → t–1m) Classic Jegadeesh-Titman: 12-month momentum skipping the most recent month to avoid short-term reversal
mom_6_1 Return(t–6m → t–1m) Shorter lookback; more responsive to recent trends
mom_3_1 Return(t–3m → t–1m) Short-term momentum
mom_1 Return(t–1m → t) Raw 1-month return; often reverses
ma_ratio Price / 200-day MA – 1 Trend-following: distance above/below long-run average
high_52w Price / 52-week high – 0.95 Proximity to annual high; >0 means within 5%
risk_adj mom_12_1 / realised vol Volatility-adjusted momentum (Sharpe-like)
tsmom sign(mom_12_1) Binary: +1 (positive trend) or –1 (negative)
Show code 
print("Metrics computed:", list(metrics.columns))
print(f"\nSample (last 5 rows):")
print(metrics.tail().round(4).to_string())
Metrics computed: ['mom_12_1', 'mom_6_1', 'mom_3_1', 'mom_1', 'ma_ratio', 'high_52w', 'risk_adj', 'tsmom']

Sample (last 5 rows):
            mom_12_1  mom_6_1  mom_3_1   mom_1  ma_ratio  high_52w  risk_adj  tsmom
date                                                                               
2026-03-09    0.1971   0.0529  -0.0070  0.0010    0.0362    0.0252    1.0313    1.0
2026-03-10    0.2132   0.0705   0.0102 -0.0195    0.0337    0.0237    1.1162    1.0
2026-03-11    0.2525   0.0732   0.0181 -0.0254    0.0317    0.0225    1.3352    1.0
2026-03-12    0.2596   0.0673   0.0163 -0.0377    0.0153    0.0077    1.3698    1.0
2026-03-13    0.2527   0.0582   0.0094 -0.0429    0.0090    0.0023    1.3328    1.0

Analytical Framework

For each metric and each forward return horizon (1m, 3m, 6m, 12m):

  1. Align: Resample the signal to month-end. Lag by one month so the signal is observable before the forward return period begins — no look-ahead bias.
  2. Bin: Divide the full sample into quintiles (Q1 = lowest signal, Q5 = highest).
  3. Measure: Compute mean forward return within each quintile.
  4. Summarise: Report the Q5–Q1 spread as a scalar measure of predictive power.

tsmom is excluded from quintile analysis (only two unique values; cannot form five bins). It is assessed via hit rate instead.

Full-Period Results

Q5–Q1 Spread Heatmap

The heatmap below shows the Q5–Q1 spread (%) across all metrics and forward horizons over the full 1993–2025 sample. Green = higher-signal months predicted higher returns; red = the opposite.

Show code 
spreads = spread_table(prices, metrics)
plot_spread_heatmap(spreads)
display(Image("data/spread_heatmap.png"))
Chart saved to data/spread_heatmap.png

Key observations:

  • mom_6_1 and ma_ratio show the strongest and most consistent positive spreads, particularly at 3m and 6m horizons.
  • mom_1 is negative at short horizons — a textbook short-term reversal effect.
  • high_52w and risk_adj show modest spreads; the former is noisy, the latter largely redundant with mom_12_1.

Quintile Return Profiles (3-Month Horizon)

The bar charts below show the full distribution — mean return for each quintile, not just the top and bottom — at the 3-month forward horizon.

Show code 
plot_quintile_bars(prices, metrics, horizon="fwd_3m")
display(Image("data/quintiles_fwd_3m.png"))
Chart saved to data/quintiles_fwd_3m.png

A well-behaved signal produces a monotone staircase from Q1 to Q5. mom_6_1 and ma_ratio come closest to this; mom_1 inverts it.

Time Dependency

A positive full-period spread can mask substantial regime variation. A signal that worked strongly in one era and failed in another is less reliable than one that is consistently positive. We examine this two ways.

5-Year Tranche Heatmap

Each cell is the Q5–Q1 spread within that 5-year window, using 20th/80th percentile cutoffs. With ~60 monthly observations per cell, estimates are more stable than year-by-year.

Show code 
plot_tranche_heatmap(prices, metrics, horizon="fwd_3m", tranche_years=5)
display(Image("data/tranche_heatmap_fwd_3m_5y.png"))
Chart saved to data/tranche_heatmap_fwd_3m_5y.png

Key observations:

  • No metric is green across all tranches — regime dependence is universal.
  • mom_6_1 is the most consistent: positive in 4 of 7 tranches, and the trend since 2013 is notably green.
  • The 2003–2012 period was broadly hostile to momentum at the index level, likely reflecting the two major bear-market reversals (dot-com, GFC).
  • high_52w is structurally weak — negative or near-zero in most periods.

Rolling 36-Month Information Coefficient

The IC (Spearman rank correlation between signal and forward return) computed over a rolling 36-month window shows how signal quality evolves continuously over time.

Show code 
# Compute rolling IC for each metric vs 3m forward return
outcome = fwd["fwd_3m"]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 2, figsize=(14, 12), sharey=False)
axes = axes.flatten()

from analytics import rolling_eval

cols = [c for c in metrics.columns if c != "tsmom"]
for i, col in enumerate(cols):
    aligned = align_monthly(metrics[col], outcome, lag=1)
    rolling_ic = rolling_eval(
        aligned["signal"], aligned["outcome"],
        information_coefficient, window=36
    )
    ax = axes[i]
    s = rolling_ic.dropna()
    ax.plot(s.index, s.values, color="steelblue", linewidth=1.2)
    ax.axhline(0, color="red", linewidth=0.8, linestyle="--")
    ax.fill_between(s.index, s.values, 0, where=s.values > 0, alpha=0.15, color="green")
    ax.fill_between(s.index, s.values, 0, where=s.values < 0, alpha=0.15, color="red")
    ax.set_title(col, fontsize=11)
    ax.set_ylabel("IC (Spearman)")
    ax.grid(alpha=0.3)

for j in range(i + 1, len(axes)):
    axes[j].set_visible(False)

fig.suptitle("36-Month Rolling IC vs 3-Month Forward Return", fontsize=13, y=1.01)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("data/rolling_ic_3m.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.close()
display(Image("data/rolling_ic_3m.png"))

The rolling IC reveals what the tranche heatmap compresses: mom_6_1 has been positively correlated with 3-month forward returns for most of the post-2013 period, with IC values persistently in the 0.1–0.3 range — economically meaningful for a single-factor signal.

Summary Statistics

Show code 
rows = []
for col in [c for c in metrics.columns if c != "tsmom"]:
    aligned = align_monthly(metrics[col], outcome, lag=1)
    s  = spread_stat(aligned["signal"], aligned["outcome"]) * 100
    ic = information_coefficient(aligned["signal"], aligned["outcome"])
    rows.append({"Metric": col, "Spread (%, 3m)": round(s, 2), "IC (Spearman)": round(ic, 3)})

summary = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("Metric").sort_values("IC (Spearman)", ascending=False)
print(summary.to_string())
          Spread (%, 3m)  IC (Spearman)
Metric                                 
mom_6_1             4.65          0.137
ma_ratio            3.49          0.068
mom_12_1            2.61          0.048
mom_3_1             2.46          0.043
risk_adj            1.93          0.021
mom_1              -0.29          0.007
high_52w            1.98         -0.012

Conclusions

Three findings stand out from the 1993–2025 analysis:

1. mom_6_1 is the most robust momentum signal at the index level. A 6-month lookback (skipping the most recent month) produces the highest IC and the most consistent Q5–Q1 spread across 5-year tranches. The signal has strengthened since 2013, not weakened — counter to common assumptions about momentum decay.

2. Short-term reversal is real but fading. mom_1 (raw 1-month return) is negatively predictive at 1-month horizon — high recent returns predict slightly lower near-term returns. However, the rolling IC trend is flat toward zero, suggesting the effect has diminished as markets became more efficient.

3. No signal is regime-stable. Every metric spent extended periods (2003–2012 in particular) with near-zero or negative spreads. This argues against using any single momentum metric as a standalone timing rule, and in favour of treating them as probabilistic inputs to a broader framework.

Analysis produced using SPY adjusted-close data via yfinance. All forward returns computed on a look-ahead-free basis with a one-month signal lag. Code available at github.com/jackmdsimmons/sp500-momentum.
